title: From peak back to target: tracking expectations during the inflation surge
summary: ECB staff economists present a Dynamic Nelson-Siegel statistical model to estimate monthly inflation expectations from the ECB Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF) and Consensus Economics survey data, which are otherwise available only quarterly. The analysis of the 2021-2022 inflation surge shows that forecasters initially underestimated both the scale and persistence of inflation, revising their 12-month outlook from 1.6% in January 2022 to 2.8% in February 2022 (post-Ukraine invasion) and peaking at 4.3% in September 2022, while medium-term expectations remained anchored around the 2% target throughout.
key_points: Authors developed a Dynamic Nelson-Siegel (DNS) model to estimate monthly inflation expectations from quarterly surveys (ECB SPF and Consensus Economics), extending forecast horizons from 15 months to up to 10 years
During July 2021 to January 2022, inflation rose from 2% to 5% while 12-month ahead forecasts remained at only 1.6%, reflecting belief that price increases were transitory
After Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, 12-month inflation expectations jumped from 1.6% to 2.8% within one month in anticipation of energy cost surge
By September 2022, just before inflation peaked in October 2022, 12-month expectations reached 4.3%, with projected return to 2% delayed by approximately one year
Longer-term forecasts (two years and beyond) remained near 2% throughout the surge, demonstrating robust medium-term anchoring
The ECB began raising key policy rates in July 2022; stable anchored expectations are credited with containing longer-run inflation pressures and facilitating quicker, less costly disinflation
why_it_matters: This research demonstrates the critical role of well-anchored inflation expectations in enabling central banks to conduct monetary policy effectively. The ECB's ability to raise rates from July 2022 was facilitated by medium-term expectations remaining anchored at 2%, suggesting that credibility built through consistent policy actions helps contain inflation dynamics even during large shocks. The methodology provides a tool for policymakers to track expectations in near real-time rather than waiting for quarterly survey releases.
macro_context: The source analyzes inflation expectations dynamics during the 2021-2022 European inflation surge, showing how the Russia-Ukraine conflict shifted expectations sharply. After inflation peaked in October 2022, forecasts for the coming 12 months and beyond stabilized around 3.6%, with projected return to target within two years. The analysis supports the view that well-anchored medium-term expectations are a precondition for smooth disinflation without requiring excessive policy tightening.
important_numbers: 5% inflation level reached by January 2022 (from 2% in July 2021)
notes: Source is a recent (2 days old) ECB staff research blog post on inflation expectations methodology. The article was truncated in extraction but the core methodology and key findings for the 2021-2022 period are fully captured. No specific forward-looking inflation expectations for current or future periods are provided in the visible text; the analysis focuses on historical dynamics. Authors are ECB economists (Böninghausen, Gosselin, Schupp, Vladu).
